launch.title: Is blockchain right for you?
launch.subtitle: Swipe through a few quick questions and find out whether — and which — blockchain actually fits your project.

intro.title: Is blockchain right for you?
intro.body: Most projects don't need a blockchain. This quick test asks a handful of honest questions, then tells you whether one would genuinely help you — and if so, which kind.

ui.swipeHint: Swipe the card, tap the buttons, or use ← / → keys

intermediary.title: The middleman
intermediary.example: Spotify keeps roughly 30% of every stream before the artist sees a cent. Blockchain can connect two parties directly, without that middleman.
intermediary.question: Is there an intermediary taking a cut or controlling access that you'd like to remove?

multiParty.title: Many hands, no boss
multiParty.example: Think of several banks, or competing logistics firms, that must agree on one shared record — but none of them trusts the others to keep the master copy.
multiParty.question: Do several organisations that don't fully trust each other need to share one single source of truth?

shareData.title: Data that must travel
shareData.example: A patient's medical history scattered across hospitals — each visit re-asks the same questions. Shared, traceable records speed everything up.
shareData.question: Do you need to share records between organisations reliably, with a clear trace of who changed what?

confidential.title: Eyes only
confidential.example: Contract prices between two companies, or patient data, can't simply be posted on a public ledger for the whole world to read.
confidential.question: Must your data stay private — visible only to authorised participants, not the whole world?

tokenize.title: Turning things into tokens
tokenize.example: A building, a bar of gold, or shares of a company split into digital tokens that anyone can buy, sell, or trade 24/7.
tokenize.question: Do you want to turn a real asset (real estate, gold, company shares) into tradable digital tokens?

publicVerify.title: Trust, but verify
publicVerify.example: A charity that lets any donor follow their money on-chain, or a product anyone can trace from farm to shelf.
publicVerify.question: Do you need anyone in the public to be able to verify your transactions independently?

immutable.title: Written in stone
immutable.example: A diploma, a property title, or an audit log that no one — not even the administrator — can quietly rewrite later.
immutable.question: Do you need a tamper-proof history that can never be altered after the fact?

centralDb.title: The honest question
centralDb.example: If one trusted organisation already controls the data and everyone is fine with that, a normal database is faster, cheaper and simpler than any blockchain.
centralDb.question: Would a normal database, under your organisation's sole control, already do the job?

not-needed.title: A classic database is probably enough
not-needed.summary: Based on your answers, your project doesn't show the friction blockchain is built to solve — no untrusted parties, no middleman to remove, no need for public verifiability. A well-built traditional database would be faster, cheaper and simpler. That's a perfectly good answer.
not-needed.why: Blockchain earns its cost when multiple parties who don't trust each other must share a tamper-proof record. When one trusted owner runs the show, that overhead buys you nothing.

permissioned.title: A permissioned blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric)
permissioned.summary: You have known partners who must share trustworthy data while keeping it confidential — classic territory for supply chains, healthcare and inter-bank settlement. A permissioned blockchain like Hyperledger Fabric gives you a shared, tamper-proof ledger that only authorised members can read and write.
permissioned.why: Your answers point to several known-but-not-fully-trusted organisations, confidential data and the need for a reliable shared trace — exactly what consortium blockchains are designed for, without exposing anything to the public.

public-evm.title: A public EVM blockchain (Ethereum, Polygon)
public-evm.summary: You want to create or trade digital assets — tokenising property, gold, shares, or building open financial products. Public EVM chains (Ethereum and its Layer-2s like Polygon) are the most liquid, composable home for smart contracts and tokenised assets.
public-evm.why: Tokenisation and open, anyone-can-participate markets need a public chain with rich smart-contract tooling. The EVM ecosystem has the widest tooling, liquidity and standards (ERC-20, ERC-721) for exactly this.

public-general.title: A public blockchain (transparency & disintermediation)
public-general.summary: Your value comes from removing a middleman and letting anyone verify what happened — public donations, traceability, censorship-resistant records. A public blockchain gives you that radical transparency and openness out of the box.
public-general.why: You prioritised public verifiability and cutting out intermediaries over confidentiality. That openness is the whole point of a public chain — start from the fundamentals to choose the right one.
